confidence.disclosure: Muhi al-Din Muhammad Aurangzeb Alamgir, the sixth Mughal emperor, is among the best documented Indian rulers. Sources include the Alamgirnama of Muhammad Kazim, the Ma'asir-i Alamgiri of Saqi Mustaid Khan, the Muntakhab al-Lubab of Khafi Khan, and Aurangzeb's own surviving letters (the Ruqa'at-i Alamgiri and Ahkam-i Alamgiri). The major events of his life - birth at Dohad, governorship of the Deccan, war of succession, accession at Delhi in 1658, the long Deccan campaigns of 1681-1707, and his death at Ahmednagar - are firm by year and many by day.

sources: Audrey Truschke, Aurangzeb: The Man and the Myth (2017)

journey.event: Born on 3 November 1618 in Dahod (Dohad) in Gujarat, the third son of Prince Khurram (the future Shah Jahan) and his Persian wife Mumtaz Mahal. His father is then in rebellion against his own father, the emperor Jahangir.

When his father's revolt fails in 1622, the young Aurangzeb and his brother Dara Shikoh are sent to the court of Jahangir as hostages, separated from their parents for several years. They live as effective prisoners at the imperial court until their father makes peace.

On 29 January 1628 his father Khurram is enthroned as the emperor Shah Jahan in Agra following Jahangir's death. The young Aurangzeb is reunited with his family and grows up at the splendid imperial court of Shah Jahan.

In 1633, at the age of fourteen, faces a charging war elephant at an imperial elephant fight. According to the famous incident, the elephant suddenly turns and charges the prince, but Aurangzeb stands his ground and strikes it on the trunk with his lance, surviving until help arrives. Shah Jahan praises him publicly. Already by his teenage years his austere, religious, and serious personality contrasts sharply with that of his elder brother Dara Shikoh.

In 1636, at the age of eighteen, is appointed viceroy of the Deccan and based at Burhanpur. He governs the Deccan provinces (Khandesh, Berar, Telangana) for the next eight years, gaining administrative and military experience and developing a deep knowledge of the politics of southern India.

Around 1645 is reassigned as governor of Gujarat. Over the next decade serves as governor of Gujarat, Multan, and twice again of the Deccan, gaining a reputation as a capable administrator and a vigorous military commander.

Returned to the Deccan as viceroy in 1653, makes Khirki (which he renames Aurangabad after himself) his capital. From here conducts campaigns against the Shia sultanates of Bijapur and Golconda. The Deccan policy that will dominate the rest of his life takes shape during this second viceroyalty.

In September 1657 his father Shah Jahan falls seriously ill in Delhi. The four sons of Shah Jahan - Dara Shikoh (the favoured eldest), Shah Shuja, Aurangzeb, and Murad Bakhsh - immediately begin manoeuvring for the succession. The war of succession that follows will be the most dramatic in Mughal history.

On 29 May 1658, at the Battle of Samugarh near Agra, Aurangzeb (with his brother Murad Bakhsh) decisively defeats the army of Dara Shikoh. The victory effectively settles the succession war. Dara flees north and west; Aurangzeb marches on Agra.

On 8 June 1658 reaches Agra and besieges his father Shah Jahan in the Red Fort. Within days Shah Jahan is forced to surrender. Aurangzeb places his father under house arrest in the fort, where he remains a prisoner until his death eight years later. Aurangzeb has Murad Bakhsh imprisoned and later executed; Dara Shikoh is captured and executed in September 1659; Shah Shuja flees to Burma where he disappears.

On 21 July 1658 (or by some accounts 31 July) is enthroned as the sixth Mughal emperor in the Shalimar Bagh near Delhi, taking the regnal name Alamgir ('World-Seizer'). A second, formal enthronement takes place in Delhi in 1659.

In June 1665 his general Mirza Raja Jai Singh (a Hindu Rajput) defeats the Maratha leader Shivaji at the Treaty of Purandar. Shivaji is brought to Aurangzeb's court at Agra in 1666 but escapes after being placed under house arrest, and resumes his rebellion. The Marathas will eventually become the empire's most persistent and successful enemies.

In 1669 issues an order for the destruction of the Keshava Deva temple at Mathura, on the supposed birthplace of the Hindu god Krishna, and builds a mosque on the site. Other temples are also targeted. The motivations and the scope of his temple destruction policies have been intensely debated by historians; some were political (against rebellious zamindars), others religious.

In April 1679 reimposes the jizya - the tax on non-Muslims - which had been abolished by Akbar in 1564. The reimposition is intensely unpopular and contributes to revolts among the Rajputs and Jats. It is one of the most controversial acts of his reign.

In September 1681 leaves Delhi for the Deccan, ostensibly to suppress the rebellion of his own son Akbar (who has joined the Rajputs and the Marathas). He never returns north. The Deccan campaigns of 1681-1707 - a continuous war of 26 years - dominate the rest of his life.

On 12 September 1686 takes the great Adil Shahi sultanate of Bijapur after a long siege. The Adil Shahi dynasty, which had ruled Bijapur for 200 years, comes to an end and Bijapur is annexed to the Mughal empire.

On 21 September 1687 takes Golconda (Hyderabad), the wealthy diamond-producing capital of the Qutb Shahi sultanate, after a brutal siege of more than seven months. The Qutb Shahi dynasty falls. With Golconda Aurangzeb has formally absorbed both Deccan sultanates - but the Maratha resistance continues with redoubled intensity.

In February 1689 captures Sambhaji, the son and successor of Shivaji and the leader of the Marathas. Sambhaji is tortured and executed at Tulapur on 11 March 1689. His death turns him into a Maratha martyr and the Maratha resistance becomes more determined than ever.

Throughout the 1690s and early 1700s besieges one Maratha hill fort after another. Now in his eighties, he personally directs sieges of Satara, Parli, Panhala, and others. Each fort takes months or years to reduce, and as soon as one falls the Marathas retake another. The Mughal treasury is drained; the army is exhausted; the empire's structure is decaying as Aurangzeb is absent from the imperial heartland for over two decades.

Dies on 3 March 1707 (28 Dhu al-Qa'da 1118 AH) at Ahmednagar in Maharashtra, aged 88, in his 50th regnal year - the longest reign in Mughal history. By his own request he is buried in a simple, open-air grave at Khuldabad in the Deccan, near the tomb of his Sufi master Shaykh Zayn al-Din. His tomb is paid for from money he earned by copying the Quran and stitching prayer caps in his last years. His death marks the end of the great age of the Mughal empire; within decades it begins to disintegrate.
